cue: Describe an important decision you made
chain: 去香港读博 vs 立刻规培 → 2025硕士快毕业 → 规培稳但想开眼界/国际化 → 纠结:生活贵、怕没offer丢一年 → 建Excel追踪导师、天天刷邮箱 → 春节收到offer、又紧张又激动 → 转折点:坚持就有结果
script: The important decision I'd like to talk about was my decision to go to Hong Kong for a PhD, instead of starting clinical residency immediately in mainland China.

I made this decision in 2025, when I was close to finishing my master's. Clinical residency seemed like a more stable, traditional path, but I also had the chance to apply for a PhD at HKU. I wanted to broaden my horizons, study in a more international environment, and learn something beyond traditional dentistry.

To be honest, it wasn't an easy decision. I was worried about the high cost of living in Hong Kong, and afraid that if I didn't get the offer, I might lose a whole year. During that time, I built an Excel sheet to track the supervisors I contacted, and checked my email almost every day.
The offer came during the Spring Festival — I felt nervous, excited and relieved all at once.
Looking back, it was a real turning point. And honestly, it taught me that if you just keep at it and don't give up, things tend to work out.

cue: Describe an app / a piece of technology you use
chain: ChatGPT+Codex → 早就用、近半年重度用Codex → 牙科博士:分析数据/处理生物医学图像/搭工作流/做demo → 用大白话描述就能变代码 → 例子:胚胎细胞分割、标小批数据训练模型、效果大好 → 改变了我的思维 → 想成为会用AI+编程解决问题的人
script: The app I'd like to talk about is ChatGPT — and more recently a coding tool called Codex. I started using ChatGPT quite early, and I've been using Codex more seriously in the past half year or so. At first I thought they were just interesting, but they've actually changed the way I do research.

As a PhD student in dentistry, I use these tools for all kinds of things — analysing data, processing biomedical images, building workflows, and making small demos. The part I really like is that I can just describe what I want in plain English, and the tool helps me turn it into code or a clear set of steps.

For example, in one project I had to deal with cell segmentation in embryo images. The original images weren't very clear, so the results were poor at first. Instead of doing everything by hand, I labelled a small batch of images, used them to train the model, and let the AI help me fix the workflow. In the end it worked a lot better.

More importantly, it kind of changed how I think. I realised that these days it's not just about one specific skill — it's about knowing how to ask good questions, break problems down, and use the tools wisely.
So honestly, these tools changed what I want to be — not just a dentist, but someone who can use AI and coding to solve real problems too.

cue: Describe a time someone helped you
chain: 师兄 → 我刚到香港很迷茫 → 带我逛西营盘 → 帮我找学校附近的房 → 哪条街上下坡/哪里吃饭便宜 → 让我没那么孤单 → 适应更快 → 关键时刻的帮助很重要
script: The person I'd like to talk about is a senior labmate in my research group at HKU. I met him when I first arrived in Hong Kong about a month ago.

Honestly, when I got here I was a bit lost — I didn't know the area at all, the rent was scary, and everything felt expensive and a little overwhelming. He didn't have to help, but he spent a whole afternoon showing me around Sai Ying Pun, the area where I live now.

He helped me find a reasonable flat close to campus, told me which streets go uphill and downhill so I wouldn't get lost, and even showed me the cheaper local places to eat. Little things, but really useful.

What helped most, though, wasn't the practical stuff — it was that he made me feel less alone in a new city. After that I settled in much faster, and I could actually focus on my research instead of worrying about daily life.
I'm really grateful to him. It taught me that a small bit of help at the right moment can make a huge difference — and now I try to do the same for new students who've just arrived.

cue: Describe a time you worked in a team
chain: 组织班级篮球赛(3男2女)→ 我管整体 → 球衣没到、借别学院队服 → 没特长生但超团结、分工(我防守+垃圾话)→ 累了互相打气 → 1+1>2 → 季军、眼泪夺眶、脚磨泡、为一个目标一起拼
script: The teamwork experience I'd like to talk about was a basketball tournament that I helped organise for my class. We had mixed teams, with three boys and two girls on court, and I was responsible for managing the whole event.

Right from the start, we had to work together. The jerseys we had ordered didn't arrive in time, so I quickly borrowed some kits from another faculty so that the teams could be told apart. Sorting that problem out together actually helped us bond early on.

What I'm most proud of is that, even though we had no outstanding players, we were extremely united. Everyone had a clear role: I took charge of defence, and I also used a bit of friendly trash-talk to distract the other team. Whenever someone got tired, we encouraged each other, and it really felt like we achieved far more as a team than we ever could have on our own.

In the end, we finished third. I was so moved that I actually teared up — my feet were covered in blisters and everyone was exhausted, but we had worked towards the same goal together. It's an experience I'll never forget.

cue: Describe a city you like / would recommend
chain: 香港 → 来一个月、还新鲜 → 拥挤/国际/贵 → 最爱:海+港大氛围+国际化 → 西营盘上下坡、能同时看到高楼和海 → 不完美不久住 → 推荐给年轻人
script: The city I'd like to talk about is Hong Kong, where I'm currently living while I do my PhD. I arrived about a month ago, so it still feels quite new to me. My first impression was that it's an extremely crowded, international and rather expensive city.

What I like most is the combination of the sea, the strong academic atmosphere at HKU, and the international environment. I get to meet people from all over the world, which has really broadened my perspective. I'm also fond of the area where I live, Sai Ying Pun, where the streets rise and fall steeply, and from a slope you can sometimes see the tall buildings and the sea at the same time.

Of course, it isn't perfect. It's crowded and fairly expensive, so I probably wouldn't settle here permanently. Even so, it's a wonderful place to experience, especially for young people who want to study and enjoy a different way of life.
Overall, I would happily recommend Hong Kong to almost anyone around my age.

chain: bridge牙科+AI → 硕士起就有、读博更清晰 → 做帮牙医诊断的AI(读片) → 尤其给小城市/像我家乡 → 怎么实现:读完博、发论文、学编程+临床 → 为什么:大小城市医疗差距 / 父亲是医生
script: The long-term goal I'd like to talk about is becoming a researcher who connects dentistry and AI. I've had this goal since my master's, but it became much clearer after I started my PhD at HKU.

What I want to do is build AI tools that can help dentists make better diagnoses, for example by reading dental scans more accurately. The part I care about most is making this useful for smaller cities, places like my hometown, where there aren't as many experienced doctors as in big cities.

As for how I'll get there, it has to be step by step. First I need to finish my PhD and publish some solid work, and then keep improving the practical side, such as coding and clinical knowledge. It's a long road, but I'm committed to it.

The main reason I set this goal is that my father is a doctor, so I grew up seeing how much good healthcare matters. During my studies I also noticed a real gap between the care available in big cities and in smaller places. If my work could narrow that gap even a little, it would mean a great deal to me.

cue: Describe a person who overcame difficulties and succeeded
chain: 表姐 → 想当医生 → 考研失败两次 → 大家劝她放弃 → 每天学十小时、坚持一年 → 第三次考上 → 现在住院医师 → 坚持比天赋重要
script: The person I'd like to talk about is my older cousin, who is someone who really inspires me. She had wanted to become a doctor ever since she was young.

The difficulty was that she failed the postgraduate entrance exam, not once but twice. It's an extremely competitive exam in China, and after the second attempt, many people around her, even some family members, suggested that she give up and find an ordinary job.

But she didn't. The way she got through it was pure persistence. She studied for around ten hours a day for a whole year, and she stayed remarkably disciplined the entire time. I was genuinely amazed at how she kept going, and on the third attempt she finally passed and got into a good program.

Now she is working as a resident doctor, which is exactly what she had always wanted. I really admire her. She taught me something important: that persistence often matters more than natural talent. Whenever I feel like giving up on my own research, I think about her.

cue: Describe a time you changed an important opinion
chain: 以前觉得AI会取代医生 → 抵触、担心 → 读博真正用了 → 发现它会出错、离不开人 → 改观:AI是辅助不是替代 → 松了口气+更开放
script: The time I'd like to talk about is when I changed my mind about AI in medicine. This was around the time I started my PhD, about a year ago.

Before that, my opinion was actually quite negative. I used to think AI would replace doctors and dentists, and that honestly made me a little anxious and resistant to it. I even wondered why I was training for this field if a machine could do the job.

What changed my mind was using AI tools in my own research. Once I worked with them every day, I realized they are far from perfect. They still make many mistakes, especially with medical images, and they really need a human to check and guide them. So I began to see AI differently, more as a tool that assists doctors rather than one that replaces them.

As for how I felt, I was relieved, and also more open-minded. It made me less afraid of new technology, and now I'm genuinely excited about working with it rather than worrying about it. I think that shift is a big reason I enjoy my research today.

chain: 发小 → 初高中同校 → 现在在悉尼 → 独立、好奇 → 自学英语/编程/AI → 看书+网课 → 不怕犯错 → 费曼学习法(用简单话讲复杂) → 我的榜样 → 好奇心+自律+持续学习
script: The person I'd like to talk about is one of my childhood friends.

We went to the same school in both junior high school and senior high school, so I've known him for many years. He is now in Sydney, so we don't meet very often, but we still keep in touch online from time to time.

What I admire most about him is that he is very independent and curious. He has taught himself many things over the years, such as English, programming, and more recently, AI. He usually learns by reading books, watching online courses, and practicing by himself.

I think he is good at learning because he is not afraid of making mistakes. Also, he often uses the Feynman technique, which means he tries to explain complicated ideas in simple language. In my opinion, if someone can explain something clearly to others, it usually means they really understand it.

When I was in junior high school, he was actually a role model for me. He always seemed to know what he wanted, and he didn't care too much about other people's opinions. That attitude had a deep influence on me, not only in study, but also in how I deal with people and make life decisions.
So although we are now in different places, I still value this friendship a lot. He reminds me that long-term growth comes from curiosity, self-discipline, and the courage to keep learning.

cue: Describe a family member you are proud of
chain: 父亲 → 农村出身 → 靠自己努力当了医生 → 离家求学、借钱读书 → 为全家拼、给下一代更好机会 → 支持我的教育、尊重我的选择 → 来港读博给我勇气 → 责任 → 教育改变未来
script: A family member I'm very proud of is my father.

He came from a rural background, but through his own effort, he became a doctor. I think this was not easy at all, especially for someone from a rural family. He had to leave home to study in another place, and at that time, the family didn't have many resources. He even had to borrow money to continue his education.

What makes me proud is not only the fact that he became a doctor, but also what he did for the whole family. He worked extremely hard, took care of family members, and gave the next generation better opportunities. In many ways, his effort changed the path of our family.

One thing that really moves me is that he always supports my education. Even when he doesn't fully understand my research or my PhD life, he respects my choices. When I decided to come to Hong Kong for my PhD, I knew it was a big step, but my family's support gave me a lot of courage.
I'm proud of him because he showed me what responsibility means. His life taught me that a person can change their future through education, hard work, and commitment to family.
